Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

sections.content:
SPACE changed the board. The white node (W) at rows 27-31, cols 16-19 now has 16 pixels (was 4 pixels before). The G node at rows 28-31, cols 16-19 disappeared. The c node at rows 32-35, cols 16-19 remains.
It seems like SPACE is filling or transforming the area. Let me try SPACE again to see if it continues to fill the target area.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: SPACE filled the G area with white. Player at rows 24-26, cols 16-19. White area now rows 27-31, cols 16-19. c area still at rows 32-35, cols 16-19.
Plan: Try SPACE again to fill the remaining c area.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try SPACE again
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE to fill remaining area')
action(['SPACE'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows=[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}] cols=[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

print("Result:", last_action_result)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
